title: How to Build Daily Study Habits That Actually Stick
lead: Building a study habit isn't about willpower—it's about making it automatic. Same time, same place, same sequence. Here's how to get there without turning it into a daily battle.

sections.title: Start Smaller Than You Think
content: An hour of study every day sounds good until day three when nobody wants to do it. Better to start with 15 minutes. Or 10. The point is to make it so easy that skipping feels sillier than doing it. Once that's automatic, you can nudge the time up.

highlights.title: The Two-Minute Rule
highlights.text: If the habit is "read for 15 minutes," the trigger might be "open the book." Just opening it counts. Usually they'll keep going. The bar has to be low enough that resistance doesn't kick in.

highlights.title: Stack It on Something Existing
highlights.text: After dinner. Right after the bus. Right before their favorite show. Attach the new habit to something that already happens. "When I sit down at the table after dinner, I do 10 minutes of math."

sections.title: Pick a Spot (And Keep It)
content: Studying from the couch one day and the kitchen table the next makes it harder for the brain to switch into work mode. A consistent spot—even if it's just a corner of the dining table—signals "this is study time." Same idea as not working from bed: place matters.

sections.title: The Same Time Most Days
content: It doesn't have to be perfect. But if Tuesday is 4pm and Wednesday is 8pm and Thursday is skipped, the habit won't form. Aim for the same general window most weekdays—e.g., after school before dinner, or right after dinner. Weekends can be looser.

list.item: Write it down: Put it on the family calendar or a whiteboard. "Study block 4:30–5pm"
Build in buffer: If school ends at 3:30, don't start at 3:35. Give them a snack, a breather, then begin
Protect the slot: Treat it like piano practice or soccer—it's a commitment, not optional

sections.title: What Counts as Study?
content: Homework. Review. Practice problems. Reading. It all counts. The habit is "I show up at this time and do academic work." What they do can vary by day—math Monday, reading Tuesday—as long as the when and where stay consistent.

sections.title: When They Push Back
content: Some resistance is normal. Keep the block short. Celebrate showing up, not just finishing. And if they're exhausted or sick, one skipped day won't wreck the habit. Consistency over perfection. The goal is to make it feel normal, not like a punishment.

cta.title: Get Support for Study Time
cta.text: A regular tutoring session can anchor the week and give structure to independent study. Try a free session with GuruForU.

faq.question: How do I start building a daily study habit?
faq.answer: Start smaller than you think—15 or even 10 minutes. Make it so easy that skipping feels sillier than doing it. Once automatic, nudge the time up.

faq.question: What is habit stacking?
faq.answer: Attach the new habit to something that already happens. 'After dinner, I do 10 minutes of math.' Same time, same place, same sequence makes it automatic.

faq.question: Does the study spot matter?
faq.answer: Yes. A consistent spot signals 'study time' to the brain. Couch one day and kitchen table the next makes it harder to switch into work mode.

faq.question: What if my child pushes back on studying?
faq.answer: Keep the block short. Celebrate showing up, not just finishing. One skipped day won't wreck the habit. Consistency over perfection—make it feel normal, not like punishment.
